and hello everyone a limitation of a lot of color education in art and design today is in its treatment of the attributes or dimensions of color color is often considered in isolation from lightness or tone and presented in terms of a single two-dimensional hue circle or color wheel very often in a form embodying historical beliefs about three primary colors even when color is considered three dimensionally it's usually with the assumption that a single set of three color attributes suffices for all purposes and for all modes of color appearance and that these three attributes can be adequately represented by a simple symmetrical model like a sphere it's been nearly 15 years since i first addressed these issues in my website the and in this talk i'll give an overview of the main alternative frameworks available to painters today for visualizing color relationships and highlight some positive developments stemming from a number of sources that have emerged over this period for painters using physical media the most widely used three-dimensional framework is the munsel system which classifies colors of paints and other physical objects in terms of the color attributes of hue lightness called value in the muscle system and chroma or strength of color what we're seeing here is the range or gamut of paint colors that can be obtained from mixtures of a particular set of acrylic paints when we look at what we might call the individual hue pages we can see that the colors reach their maximum chroma at different values for different hues forming an irregular solid that can't be reduced to a regular sphere without either distorting the chroma dimension or leaving out many paint mixtures painters learn to think of paint mixtures as following paths through this color space and it's essential for them to understand that the space is irregular and varies with different sets of paints an example of a useful procedure for using this framework is to match a target color in paint by selecting paints either side of the target color in hue and then to raise or lower them to the value of the target color before mixing these to obtain the required hue and then if necessary reducing the chroma by adding a neutral gray paint of the same value these examples have been screenshots obtained using zolt kovax vagina's program drop to color which generates 3d plots of paint mixing paths and gamuts in muscle space this free program became publicly available not long after the launch of dimensions of color and it's a wonderful resource for helping students to visualize mixtures of paints as following paths through hue value chroma space this short movie will give you a feel for the three-dimensional output of the program however all young art and design students today also need to understand digital color and while muscle notations can be converted to and from digital color specifications this is only by going through a table rather than directly through a formula for digital painters and in the digital environment generally it's more convenient to use the color space c lab known as lab space in photoshop c-lab was designed to arrange digital colors similarly to the muscle system although it specifies them in terms of reddish versus greenish and yellowish versus bluish chroma components a star and b star instead of hue and overall chroma another very useful tool for explaining color is color inspector 3d which can be used to display colors from a photograph in c lab and other color spaces it's actually been around since 2004 although i only came across it a few years ago again this short movie will give you a feeling for the three-dimensional output of the program and how it can be used to help students to visualize paint mixing paths in hue value chroma space so for example we can compare the gamut or range of mixtures obtained from different sets of paints and here we see plotted the changes in hue and chroma as well as where lightness increases going up the page and chroma increases to the right but there's another way we can think of variations of color on this page we can consider these colors to be perceptually component and towards the right corner they're perceived to have a high colored component this is the basis of the scandinavian natural color system or ncs in which these components are assigned numbers taken to add up to one hundred colors are specified in the ncs according to their perceived black content called blackness and their perceived color content called chromaticness or in standard cie terms chroma relative to the maximum chroma possible for the hue if we were to arrange the triangles for every hue around a central axis we'd get a symmetrical colour space in the form of a double three less saturated orange to our eyes from the same planes of this cube to paint the appearance of this scene we would in some way translate these differences in brightness and colorfulness into pixels as colors of lights s is a simple index intended to predict the saturation of the digital color relative to the maximum saturation possible for that hue and b is intended to predict the brightness of the color relative to the maximum brightness possible for digital colors of that hue and saturation and on its right hand side the adobe photoshop color picker also provides an alternative way of specifying colors of pixels as object colors having lightness using lab space blackness-based specifications are not as readily available in the digital realm at present but a complete set of ncs colors can be downloaded as a free swatch file from the ncs website and a color space specifying digital colors in terms of relative whiteness and relative blackness has recently become available in css just as different three-dimensional color frameworks are useful for different purposes different hue circles may also be needed for different purposes a minority of painters refer to a physical color atlas usually the muscle book of color to specify paint use but many more make use of one or more hue circles as a conceptual framework i'll briefly outline the main hue circles that painters today think of in terms of the munsell hue circle is structured around five opposite pairs of hues so it's a bit more complicated to learn than the three opposite pairs of many popular color wheels but it does have the advantage of being perceptually even and of showing to a very good approximation additive or visual complementaries for example showing red opposite turquoise or blue green and green opposite magenta or red purple when a simpler conceptual framework is all that's required a basic hue circle structured around the unique hues arranged in opponent pairs of yellow opposite blue and red opposite green may suffice neither the precise hues deemed to be elementary red yellow green and blue in the ncs nor the slightly different hues conventionally regarded as middle red yellow green and blue by painters are evenly spaced perceptually but this may not be a significant drawback for many purposes the appeal of the traditional red yellow and blue primary colours is understandable in the context of the concept of unique hues which holds that red yellow and blue along with green are perceived to be the pure components that combine to form intermediate hue perceptions as i argued last year in my presentation for color connections the emission of green from the traditional primary colors can be seen as a relic of the view that prevailed before we understood subtractive mixture when the color green was thought to be physically composed of yellow and blue emitting green and placing middle red yellow and blue at equal distances results in a hue circle that is perceptually very uneven and in which some of the opposing hues are far from visual complementaries despite these drawbacks some very skilled painters continue to favor the traditional colour wheel as a simple practical framework although one suspects that an equally simple but less problematic framework might serve their purposes even better the most unfortunate aspect of the continued use of the red yellow blue framework is that it encourages misconceptions about color itself it can take considerable effort to dislodge from students mind the idea that a greenish yellow contains some blue or a greenish blue contains some yellow once it's in there if we replace the middle red and middle blue of the traditional color wheel with magenta and cyan respectively we can obtain a hue circle that is less distorted in perceptual spacing and in complementary relationships further to this a cyan magenta and yellow palette has a larger and more even gamut than other sets of colorant paints including the three traditional primaries red yellow and blue but what's more important is the reason why this is so a distinct difference in the pattern of mixing paths for paints close to cyan magenta and yellow hues compared to paints distant from these hues you'll notice that these red paints make high chroma mixtures only with paints that are quite close in hue and that they're neutralized by paints of a quite a wide range of opposing hues but now as we see these yellow paints we're seeing that we get quite high chroma mixtures with paints that are quite distant in numerous skilled exponents of the muscle system as applied to painting but was also a home for high level discussion and exchange of information about all aspects of our current understanding of
